Tafseer:
The nature of the use of 'la' before an oath:
This Arabic style of grammar has been explained in various places—namely, that when 'la' appears before an oath as it does here, it is not used to negate the oath itself, but rather to negate the listener's assumption which this oath intends to refute! Just as this style is common in Arabic, it is also well known in our language. When you want to immediately refute someone's statement, you say: 'No, by God, the actual reality is this.' This style of oath demonstrates that in the eyes of the speaker, the listener's assumption is so absurd that the speaker is not willing to delay its refutation even for a moment by waiting until after the oath. Rather, he deems it necessary to express his refutation, and indeed his loathing, before it. Some have regarded this 'la' as redundant, while others have connected it to the verb, but both opinions are contrary to Arabic grammar. We have refuted this in various places in this book. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) also discussed this in detail in his commentary on Surah Al-Qiyamah; refer to it for details.
Here, the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh) is not mentioned. There are two reasons for this.
The eloquence of omitting the subject of the oath: First, the subject of the oath is so clear here that there is no need to express it. It is as if the oath itself serves as the proof of its subject—the sun itself is the proof of the sun. Various examples of this have appeared in the previous Surahs. In Surah Qaf and Surah Sad, the oaths 'By the honored Qur'an' and 'By the Qur'an containing the reminder' also appeared without an explicit subject. This kind of oath is intended to show the listener that what he is denying is such a true witness to its own truthfulness that there is no room to deny it.
Second, the subsequent oath by the 'self-accusing soul' (nafs-i-lawwamah) is such an obvious proof of the truth of the Resurrection that denying the Resurrection is equivalent—as will be explained—to denying one's own heart and conscience. With this testimony, the Resurrection does not remain dependent on any external proof; rather, it becomes both the claim and the proof, the oath and the subject of the oath.

The testimony of the self-accusing soul to the Resurrection:
This is the second oath, and its subject is also omitted. The reason is that the subject is already implied within the oath itself. The meaning is: the existence of the self-accusing soul within man is a testimony that the Resurrection is true. Thus, this second oath, within the structure of the oath itself, clarifies both the claim and the proof, pointing to the reality that the Resurrection does not depend on any external proof. Its reflection is present within every person's inner self, and he witnesses it, no matter how much he tries to argue against it.
'Nafs-i-lawwamah' (the self-accusing soul) does not refer to a separate and independent soul, but is an aspect of the human soul. Allah Almighty structured the human soul in such a manner that He endowed it with the awareness of both goodness and evil, and established the rule for its success and ruin: whoever purifies his soul from evils will succeed, and whoever corrupts it will fail. In Surah Ash-Shams, He states:
(And [by] the soul and He who proportioned it and inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness. He has succeeded who purifies it, and he has failed who instills it [with corruption].) (Ash-Shams, 91:7-10).
Due to this structure, the soul occasionally becomes overwhelmed by its desires, losing its balance and inciting man to evil. This tendency of the soul is described in the Qur'an as 'nafs-i-ammarah' (the soul that incites to evil). Prophet Joseph (peace be upon him) pointed to this aspect of the soul:
(And I do not acquit myself. Indeed, the soul is a persistent enjoiner of evil...) (Yusuf, 12:53).
However, this soul also possesses the awareness of virtues; therefore, as long as its balance is maintained, it blames itself if any evil is committed by it, and feels grieved and blames others upon seeing their evils. This aspect of the soul is described here as 'nafs-i-lawwamah' (the self-accusing soul).
The way to maintain the soul's balance: The method Allah Almighty taught to keep the soul's balance stable is that a person should constantly remember his Lord and the Day of Recompense. This remembrance keeps the soul's balance stable, preventing it from being so overwhelmed by desires that it surrenders completely. If any lapse occurs occasionally, the self-accusing soul immediately corrects it, and the person, taking heed, tries to erase this stain through repentance. The soul that attains this balance is described in the Qur'an as 'nafs-i-mutma'innah' (the satisfied soul). This is the highest status of self-reform, to attain which Allah Almighty invited man and made arrangements through the Shariah. This very soul will attain the status of 'pleased and pleasing' in the Hereafter, which is the pinnacle of the human soul.
The awareness of evil as evil is endowed within human nature: This clarifies that the awareness of evil as evil is endowed within human nature from day one. Abel's brother Cain, overwhelmed by envy, killed his brother, but after killing him, he tried to hide his body. This attempt, obviously, had to be made because he felt the act was a sin. Even the worst person commits a sin not because he regards it as goodness, but because he is overwhelmed by emotions and desires. If he excuses his own sin, he does so contrary to his nature; for if someone else commits the same evil against him, he regards it as evil and protests against it. If you examine the conscience of the bad, you will find that in their hearts, respect and honor belong only to goodness, even if their practice is contrary to it. Since man adopted social and collective life, he has inevitably established a system to maintain truth and justice. Although occasionally certain evils have overwhelmed society, suppressing virtues, the collective conscience of society has never been pleased with this; rather, there have always been those who performed the same duty within society as the self-accusing soul performs within every true-natured person. If the matter exceeded this limit—meaning not a single spark of goodness remained—then the law of nature erased that society from the face of the earth.
Some questions and their answers: Now, the question is: since man possesses a guardian within his own conscience who corrects him for the evils he commits, how can it be rational to assume that he is like an unbridled beast, allowed to live whatever life he chooses and oppose this guardian as much as he wants, without anyone having the right to question him? If man is an unbridled beast, where did this self-accusing soul come from? If his Creator is unconcerned with his goodness and evil, why did He place this constant friction within man to praise goodness and rebuke evil? Then, another question arises: since He has established this small court within every human being, why should He not establish a supreme court for this entire universe to account for the good and evil deeds of the entire world, and recompense everyone in accordance with their deeds? Anyone who reflects on these questions free of desires will answer that indeed, man's own existence is a witness that he was created with the awareness of good and evil, he is not an unbridled beast, but a Day of questioning is bound to come when he will be punished for his evils if he committed them, and rewarded for his virtues if he performed them. To remind of this Day, the Creator placed a small model of it within man's own soul, so that man does not remain heedless of it, and if heedlessness occurs occasionally, he can look within his own soul to see its reflection. This reality has been explained by wise men and gnostics: man is a microcosm containing the full reflection of the macrocosm; if man recognizes himself correctly, he recognizes God and the Hereafter. Socrates' famous maxim is: 'O man, know thyself!'

The deniers' fabrication to flee from reality:
Although the word 'insan' (human) is general, the addressees are those deniers of the Resurrection among the Quraysh whose doubts have been discussed in the previous Surahs. To express indifference to them, the address uses a general word. It is stated: while the testimony to prove the Resurrection is present within every person, and there is no need to look far, these people assume that after they die and turn to dust, We will be unable to assemble their bones. It is stated: if they regard this as impossible and are denying the Resurrection contrary to the testimony of their own conscience, they should remember that We will not only assemble their bones, but will do so with such perfection that We will restore their very fingertips. 'Banan' means fingertips. The meaning is: not even the smallest and most minute part will be left out due to any inability on Our part. 'Qadireen' is a circumstantial clause (hal) derived from the plural pronoun of 'najma'a' (We assemble).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 3 and 4 was combined in the original text.]

That is, denying the Resurrection on the ground that assembling decayed bones seems impossible is merely a fabrication to flee from reality. The actual reality is that they have become such slaves to their desires that they wish to commit sins right in front of the guardian God has established, who is not far away but is sitting within them. They are like a thief who commits theft in the presence of the police officer.
'Amamahu' (before him) has been generally interpreted to mean that a person wishes to continue committing sins throughout his remaining life, and therefore seeks excuses to deny the Resurrection. However, this interpretation neither establishes a connection with the testimony of the self-accusing soul, nor does it establish a proof against his attitude. In our view, 'before him' means: a person wishes to commit sins right in front of his conscience and his self-accusing soul, contrary to its reminders and corrections. The greatest testimony to the Resurrection is present within man's own soul, but what can be done for a person who rises to deny his own self!
In this lies the element of proof: while a person's conscience is sufficient to establish the proof of the Resurrection, a person who is determined to lie in front of his own self cannot be stopped.
This also reveals that one who commits an evil contrary to his self-accusing soul—or in other words, his conscience—commits that evil right in front of God. This is because the conscience, as noted, is the guardian and judge appointed by God; so whoever commits evil in front of it, commits evil in front of God.

A foolish demand and its moderate answer:
This describes the audacity and defiance of the deniers of the Resurrection: despite the fact that God's guardian is present within them and they are experiencing it, when warned of the Resurrection, they demand: 'Where is the Resurrection? When will it come! If it is to come, why does it not arrive! We have grown tired of hearing these warnings, but it has not arrived; so we are no longer going to be intimidated by these warnings. Those who warn of it should bring it and show it to us, only then will we accept it. We will not accept it merely on the strength of verbal warnings.'

That is, today they are hastening it as if they are fully prepared to face it, but when they face its terror, they will cry out: 'Where can we flee today?'
Since demanding to see the Resurrection is a completely foolish demand, it was not addressed directly here; instead, some aspects of its terror were presented. It is stated: on that Day, sights will be dazed, the moon will be eclipsed, and the sun and moon—which currently move in their separate orbits—will have their boundaries broken and will be joined together.
These are the conditions of the Resurrection, belonging to the allegorical verses. Comprehending their actual reality in this world is not possible. What is intended is to show: for a Day that will be so terrifying that the sun and moon will leave their orbits and be joined together, who can estimate its terror! The meaning is: if you possess any trace of intellect, seek refuge from it and choose the path shown to you to protect yourselves from its terrors, instead of hastening it.
It should noted that the conditions of the Resurrection described here are intended merely to give a basic concept, representing only a few of its countless conditions. Further on in the Surahs of this group, other aspects will appear, representing only a few of its aspects, as language is unable to describe them fully.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7, 8, and 9 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7, 8, and 9 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7, 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]

This will be the answer to their question: 'Where is the place of escape?' They will cry out: 'Where can we flee today?' and they will be told: 'No! There is no refuge.' On that Day, the place of rest will be only to your Lord. Every other path of escape will be closed on that Day.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11 and 12 was combined in the original text.]

The purpose of the Resurrection:
This states the purpose of this Day. It is stated: on that Day, everyone will be informed of what they sent before and what they left behind. Informing means that the results of their entire deeds will be laid before them. The evils they committed during their life in the world will appear, and the consequences of the virtues they ignored will also appear. The Qur'an states that on that Day, those who were heedless of the Hereafter will beat their heads, saying: 'O, would that we had sent before some deeds for this Day!' and: 'O, would that we had not deviated from the warning of the messengers, but had believed in their call.' The words 'qaddama' (sent before) and 'akhkhara' (left behind) encompass all their evil deeds, omissions, and deviations.
It should noted that to succeed in the Hereafter, a person must perform many virtues and abandon many evils. However, those who are heedless or deniers of the Hereafter remain heedless of the deeds that serve as provision for that Day, spending their entire lives in accumulating what will lead to their ruin. This verse contains a reminder and warning for such deprived individuals.

Man is a witness against himself:
This explains the reality stated in: 'Rather, man wants to sin right in front of himself'. There, in connection with the deniers' question 'He asks, "When is the Day of Resurrection?"', the discourse turned to the depiction of the Resurrection. Following that, the original discourse resumes to complete the point. It is stated: no matter how many excuses a person makes to flee from the Resurrection, he is a clear witness and proof against his own self. 'Baseerata-ala nafsihi' means: 'a witness against himself' (shahidun 'ala nafsihi). The proof of this was explained above: the self-accusing soul within man is the witness to the Resurrection; there is no need to look far. He can see its image within the mirror of his own conscience.

'Ma'adheer' is the plural of 'ma'dhirah', originally 'ma'adhir', with an added 'ya' just like in 'manakeer'. It means false excuses and vain pretexts. The Arabic proverb states: (Excuses are lies). Some have regarded it as the plural of 'mi'dhar', which in the dialect of the people of Yemen means a veil. In our view, this is incorrect, as the Qur'an was revealed in the standard, standard language of the Quraysh, not in the dialect of the people of Yemen.

The background of the verses:
This exhorts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to remain patient and wait, contrary to the haste and demands of the opponents. The primary support the Prophet had to fulfill the heavy responsibility of warning lay solely in the divine revelation. He was like a soldier on the battlefield, unable to take any step without Allah's permission. The opponents, to exasperate him, raised various objections and demands to force him back. Above, their demand was mentioned: 'Where is the Resurrection of which you warn? If it is certain, why does it not arrive immediately!' Similarly, their demand is recorded elsewhere: 'If the Qur'an is the word of Allah, why is it not revealed all at once.' In short, they bombarded him with objections, and to answer them, he had to wait for the divine revelation. It was the revelation that strengthened his heart, revived his spirit, guided his intellect, and stabilized his resolve. Thus, the Qur'an and Hadith indicate that whenever the revelation was delayed due to divine wisdom, his eyes would repeatedly turn toward the heaven in anticipation. This same desire and anxiety was displayed by him when Gabriel delivered the revelation: like an eager seeker, he wanted to listen to the entire revelation immediately and preserve it perfectly, lest any drop of this rain of mercy be lost. Keep this introduction in mind as you reflect on the verses.
The Prophet's state of eagerness and anxiety during the descent of revelation: 'Move not your tongue with it to hasten [your recitation of] it' (la tuharrik bihi lisanaka li-ta'jala bih) - The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was protected from the haste and anxiety that overtook him when the revelation descended. Although eagerness and haste are common human emotions, who can describe the level of eagerness and anxiety that overtook the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) when, after a long delay and amid the storm of the opponents' mocking, Gabriel appeared with the message of his Lord! A hungry child, when embraced by his mother, wants to suckle all the milk of her breast in a single breath. A traveler in the desert, parched with thirst, when given a bucket of water after a long wait, wants to drink the entire bucket at once. A lover, after a long separation, when receiving the letter of his beloved, wants to read every word of it in a single glance. Although these examples are imperfect, they help estimate the eagerness, haste, and anxiety that must have been displayed by him when he was blessed with the revelation.
The cause was not single, but multiple:
o His entire official mission and program were communicated through it.
o His intellectual, spiritual, and religious life depended on it.
o It provided guidance to face current and future circumstances.
o It provided decisive answers to the critics' objections.
o His extraordinary desire for knowledge and deep sense of responsibility to preserve it was also a major cause.
While each of these was a noble motive, divine wisdom required that the Qur'an be revealed gradually. Thus, he was repeatedly exhorted to remain patient and wait. In Surah Ta-Ha, verses 114-115, a similar exhortation was given, and we explained some of its aspects there. Here too, the same is mentioned, but keeping in view the context, he is reassured that he should remain completely secure regarding the preservation of the Qur'an. Its collection, arrangement, recitation, memorization, and explanation of its unclear passages have been taken by Allah upon Himself. He should content himself with whatever portion is revealed, without displaying any haste, and without being anxious about its preservation. He should leave these matters to his Lord; every task will be completed at its correct time, in accordance with His wisdom and plan.

'Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation' (inna 'alayna jam'ahu wa qur'anah) - This removes the Prophet's anxiety noted above: since a great heavenly treasure was being delivered to his custody, it was natural that while receiving it, he tried to preserve every word so that not a single letter was lost. Allah Almighty reassured him: 'Preserving it and reciting it is Our responsibility.'
Collecting and arranging the Qur'an and the promise of its preservation: The word 'jam'' (collection) is comprehensive. It refers to preserving it within the Prophet's chest, as well as arranging these scattered pearls into a single string. Thus, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was constantly guided by Allah Almighty to arrange the verses revealed on different occasions within their respective Surahs. Guided by this, he directed the scribes of revelation to arrange the verses, specifying their places within the Surahs, and they executed his command.
Furthermore, Allah Almighty arranged that during every Ramadan, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) would revise the revealed Qur'an with Gabriel, ensuring no oversight or forgetfulness could occur. Narrations state that during the final Ramadan of his life, he revised it twice; the word 'qur'anah' (its recitation) points to this.

'So when We have recited it [to you], then follow its recitation' (fa-idha qara'nahu fa-ttabi' qur'anah) - That is: do not hasten the revelation on your part. Leave this to your Lord. He will reveal as much as He wills in His wisdom, and will arrange its preservation and collection. Your responsibility is only to follow the recitation of whatever We have recited. Read it, act upon it, and call to it. Pay no attention to the demand of those who ask for the entire Qur'an to be revealed all at once.

'Then indeed, upon Us is its explanation' (thumma inna 'alayna bayanah) - Furthermore, he is reassured: if any passage of the Qur'an requires explanation, there is no need to worry. Its explanation is also Our responsibility. When the time comes, We will explain it. This points to the verses revealed to explain, specify, mitigate, or complete a previous command. We have pointed to these explanatory verses in various places, and clarified that they are often followed by: (Thus Allah makes clear His verses for the people) to show that this is the fulfillment of the promise He made in: 'Then indeed, upon Us is its explanation'.
Some insights from our teacher: Regarding these verses, our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote: 
'Commentators believe that the haste mentioned in these verses was because the Prophet feared some portion of the Qur'an might be lost. We do not disagree, but some details must be understood.'
'When the Prophet received the revelation, he felt it was a great responsibility and trust, and if any minor lapse occurred or a single letter was lost, he would be accountable. Along with this, he desired that it increase, hoping his nation might be guided by its blessing. Both aspects were clear; hence, the reassurance given in this Surah keeps both aspects in view.'
'Allah Almighty has promised the preservation of the Qur'an both generally and specifically. For example:
(And indeed, it is a mighty Book. Falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it) (Fussilat, 41:41-42).
Elsewhere, He states:
(Indeed, it is We Who sent down the message and indeed, We will be its guardian) (Al-Hijr, 15:9).
These verses clearly show that there is no possibility of any increase, decrease, or alteration in the Qur'an. This aligns with the promise of preservation He made in the verses under discussion. Thus, the entire Ummah agrees that the Qur'an is preserved. The belief attributed to the Imamiyyah that some portion of the Qur'an was lost is contrary to the explicit statements of their prominent scholars. Al-Murtadha, Abu Ja'far at-Tusi, Abu Ali at-Tabarsi (author of Majma' al-Bayan), and Muhammad bin Ali bin Babawayh al-Qummi have all strongly refuted this baseless belief. Al-Qummi states: "Our belief is that the Qur'an Allah revealed to the Prophet is identical to the one in the hands of the Ummah today, without a single letter's difference. Whoever attributes to us that we believe it is more than this is a liar." Regarding the reports they have in this regard, Al-Murtadha states: "The difference of opinion reported from some among the Imamiyyah and Hashwiyyah is of no weight. It is based on some weak reports they regarded as sound, whereas such reports cannot deny what is established with certainty."'
'Our teacher derived some conclusions from the verse under discussion:
o The Qur'an was collected and arranged in a specific order during the Prophet's life. If this promise were to be fulfilled after his death, he would not have been commanded to follow its recitation: (So when We have recited it, then follow its recitation).
o He was commanded to recite the Qur'an in the exact order it was recited to him after its collection and arrangement... This requires that the Prophet recited the Qur'an to the Ummah in the order of its final recitation, which is the order in the Preserved Tablet.
o This also reveals that after this collection and arrangement, Allah Almighty revealed the verses of specification, mitigation, or completion.'
Our teacher further writes:
'All of this is established from the Qur'an and confirmed by reports. The Prophet recited complete Surahs to the people, which is not possible unless they were recited to him in this specific order. The companions listened to it in this order, preserved it, and observed it. It is also known that he directed the scribes to write the verses at specific places within the Surahs, and they executed his command. When an explanatory verse was revealed, he directed it to be written at its specified place—either placed next to the verses it explained, or at the end of the Surah if it related to the Surah's general theme.'
'These explanatory verses also contain internal signs showing they were revealed as explanations—often accompanied by: "Thus Allah makes clear His verses for the people."'
'Similarly, reliable reports state that at the end of his life, Gabriel recited the entire Qur'an to the Prophet in its actual order, which dispels many doubts regarding the arrangement of the Qur'an.' (Excerpt from Tafseer of Surah Al-Qiyamah by Imam Farahi).

After exhorting the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to remain patient and wait, the discourse connects back to its original theme. Addressing the deniers of the Resurrection, it is stated: your attitude toward the Resurrection is not because there is no proof of it before you, as its greatest testimony is present within your own conscience. Rather, the actual cause is that you are in love with the world and its immediate pleasures, and you lack the courage to sacrifice this cash for the promise of the Hereafter.

The actual cause behind denying the Resurrection:
'And you leave the Hereafter' (wa tadharuna-l-akhirah) means: you are ignoring the Hereafter. The meaning is: the Hereafter is not hidden from you, but the world is immediate; therefore, you are infatuated with it, and since the Hereafter is not immediate, you are knowingly ignoring it.

The actual reality: you are ignoring the Hereafter in pursuit of the world, but do not forget that what you are ignoring is bound to come, and on that Day, the situation will be completely different. The faces of those who lived their lives keeping it in view will be radiant and fresh, looking forward to their Lord's mercies; and the faces of those who ignored it will be dark and distorted, realizing that a back-breaking calamity is about to befall them.
This portrays the situation before entering Paradise or Hell. When the companions of Paradise see the angels welcoming them with peace, respect, and honor at every step, their faces will be radiant at the prospect of their bright future, looking forward to the perfect manifestation of the Lord's mercy promised to them. Conversely, the treatment the disbelievers face will make their faces dark and distorted, realizing that the back-breaking calamity they were warned of but ignored is about to befall them.

The true meaning of 'Looking to their Lord' (ila rabbiha nadhirah):
'Ila rabbiha nadhirah' means they will be looking forward to the mercy and favor of their Lord. When 'nadhar' (looking) is connected with 'ila', it means looking at something, and also means looking forward to someone's mercy and favor. The lexicographers state: if someone says to a person of influence: 'Indeed, we are looking only to Allah and then to you' (innama nandhuru ila-llahi thumma ilayk), it means: we are looking forward to the favor of Allah and then your favor. (Footnote 1)
Our stance regarding seeing Allah (ru'yah): Some have used 'ila rabbiha nadhirah' to argue for the physical seeing of Allah. However, in our view, as clear from its interpretation and context, this verse is not related to that issue, which belongs to a completely different context. Similarly, those who denied the physical seeing of Allah and altered the meaning of 'ila' out of theological zeal are also incorrect. Our stance regarding seeing Allah is: in this world, our faith is in the unseen, and we see our Lord through His signs; but in the Hereafter, our faith will be through witnessing, and we will attain absolute certainty (haqq-ul-yaqeen) regarding every reality. As for the question of the nature of this witnessing, its reality cannot be known in this world. This belongs to the allegorical verses, in which prying is not permissible. Allah alone knows the nature of this witnessing.
_____
Footnote 1: Aqrab al-Mawarid.

The actual reality: you are ignoring the Hereafter in pursuit of the world, but do not forget that what you are ignoring is bound to come, and on that Day, the situation will be completely different. The faces of those who lived their lives keeping it in view will be radiant and fresh, looking forward to their Lord's mercies; and the faces of those who ignored it will be dark and distorted, realizing that a back-breaking calamity is about to befall them.
This portrays the situation before entering Paradise or Hell. When the companions of Paradise see the angels welcoming them with peace, respect, and honor at every step, their faces will be radiant at the prospect of their bright future, looking forward to the perfect manifestation of the Lord's mercy promised to them. Conversely, the treatment the disbelievers face will make their faces dark and distorted, realizing that the back-breaking calamity they were warned of but ignored is about to befall them.

The context of the discourse supports this meaning. The mental state of those entering Hell is described as: 'realizing that there will be done to them a back-breaking calamity' (tadhunnu an yuf'ala biha faqirah). That is: seeing the circumstances, they will realize that a back-breaking calamity is about to befall them, and their faces will be distorted in panic. In contrast to their state, the believers will be looking forward to the greatest mercy, and their faces will be radiant.
Zamakhshari explained the grammar of 'an yuf'ala biha faqirah' as:
('meaning: they will realize they are facing a punishment whose severity is back-breaking').
Although other grammatical structures are possible, I prefer this one. Some examples will appear in the subsequent Surahs.
'Faqirah' refers to a calamity that breaks the backbone.

Reminding the lovers of worldly pleasure of death and the Hereafter:
This reminds the lovers of worldly pleasure of the agony of death and their helplessness at that moment—showing that they should not regard the Resurrection as impossible. It will surely come, and you will have to travel to God on the Day when all your strength is gone and your shins are joined. It is better to run to God and prepare some provision before the soul reaches the throat and the shins are joined.
Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote: 
'In "When it reaches the collarbones" (balaghati-t-taraqee), the pronoun refers to the soul (nafs), which is omitted. A similar omission exists in Surah Al-Waqi'ah: (Then why, when it reaches the throat) (56:83). Such omission is well known in Arabic, and therefore explicitly mentioning "nafs" was not necessary. Classical Arab poets also used this. Hatim at-Ta'i wrote:
"O Mawiyah, of what use is wealth when the soul reaches the throat and the chest is choked"
where the subject of "hashrajat" (reaches the throat) is the soul, but it was omitted. Similar omissions exist in the Qur'an, as in: (He would not leave upon its back any creature) (Fatir, 45), where the reference of the pronoun is the earth (al-ard), which is omitted.' (Excerpt from Tafseer of Surah Al-Qiyamah by Imam Farahi).

Reminding the lovers of worldly pleasure of death and the Hereafter:
This reminds the lovers of worldly pleasure of the agony of death and their helplessness at that moment—showing that they should not regard the Resurrection as impossible. It will surely come, and you will have to travel to God on the Day when all your strength is gone and your shins are joined. It is better to run to God and prepare some provision before the soul reaches the throat and the shins are joined.
Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote: 
'"And it is said, 'Who is a healer?'" (wa qeela man raq) - This phrase expresses the severity and critical nature of the situation, and the passive voice "qeela" (it is said) is highly eloquent. It will be such a severe moment that no one will pay attention to the identity of the speaker; rather, everyone's tongue will utter this very phrase. Before a singular indefinite noun, "man" (who) is used to express either intense searching or total despair. Tarafah wrote:
"When the nation calls out: 'Who is a young man [to help]!' I assume they mean me, so I do not behave lazily."
Let us see what is intended by this style here. In our view, there are two possible interpretations, with only a minor difference:
The first is that when the agony of death begins and the soul is choked in the chest, the caretakers will cry out in panic: "Is there any healer or spiritual healer who can cure this dying person!"
The second is that they will say: "The matter is finished! Who can heal him now?" This is an expression of total despair, and hearing this, the patient will realize that his time has come. The famous poetess Khansa expressed this as:
"When the arrows of death strike someone, neither the skill of a physician can heal him, nor the breath of a healer"
These are the two interpretations; we have presented both, and you may choose either, but the second seems more aligned with the flow of the text.' (Excerpt from Tafseer of Surah Al-Qiyamah by Imam Farahi).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 27 and 28 was combined in the original text.]

Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote: 
'Regarding "And the shin is joined with the shin" (wa-l-taffati-s-saqu bis-saq), he wrote:
"Joining of the shins means a person can no longer walk, occurring due to extreme weakness and helplessness. As long as a person is alive and strong, he moves about in every field; when he dies, his shins are joined."
To express weakness and helplessness, the joining of the shins is a highly appropriate metaphor. The meaning is: when the physician despairs, the relatives surrender, the limbs fail, and he must travel to his Lord carrying a heavy burden, with no helper; what will be his state at that moment?
Some have interpreted 'saq' as 'severity of the situation', but this is the opinion of those who lack deep understanding of the Arabic language. They do not distinguish between the parts and the whole. While 'kashf 'an-is-saq' as a whole is indeed used for eagerness and activity, when these two words appear separately, 'kashf' means uncovering and 'saq' means the shin. They do not carry that idiomatic meaning when used separately.
A report from Ibn Abbas states that 'saq' refers to the final day of this world and the first day of the Hereafter. In our view, the narrators suffered some confusion. If the report is sound, it describes the situation rather than being the linguistic interpretation of 'saq'.' (Excerpt from Tafseer of Surah Al-Qiyamah by Imam Farahi).

Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote: 
'Having understood the true meaning of the joining of the shins, the beauty of "To your Lord, that Day, is the driving" (ila rabbika yawma'idhini-l-masaq) becomes clear. This is a rebuke for his negligence regarding the preparation for this journey—showing that he remained busy in the pursuit of the world until all his strength was exhausted, and now he must travel to his Lord, so how will he cover this journey!' (Excerpt from Tafseer of Surah Al-Qiyamah by Imam Farahi).

A difficult journey and no provision:
This describes the deprivation of the deniers of the Hereafter—showing that while they face a highly difficult journey, they possess no provision. They neither spent in the way of Allah nor prayed, whereas these two are what benefit on this journey.
After 'saddaqa' (believed/declared truthful), 'bi-l-husna' (in the best recompense) is omitted based on context. In Surah Al-Layl, He states:
(As for he who gives and fears Allah and believes in the best [recompense], We will ease him toward ease) (Al-Layl, 92:5-7).
It should noted that spending in the cause of Allah is a difficult pass for those who do not believe in the Hereafter and its best recompense. This pass is broken through only by those whose hearts are assured that what they spend in the world will be returned to them as an eternal treasure in the Hereafter. Belief in the recompense is what inspires spending; those who deny it never open their hands for spending. In Surah Al-Layl, He states:
(But as for he who is stingy and considers himself self-sufficient and denies the best [recompense], We will ease him toward difficulty) (Al-Layl, 92:8-10).
In the light of these verses, 'He did not believe' (fa-la saddaq) means: he neither believed in the best recompense of the Hereafter nor spent in the cause of his Lord. Thus, the word implies both the denial of the Hereafter and miserliness. After this, He stated: 'nor did he pray' (wa la salla). Both spending and prayer are inspired by belief in the recompense, and when this belief is absent, how can they exist.
Recall also that prayer and spending are the fundamental deeds upon which the Shariah is established. Now, it is clear that both depend on belief in the Hereafter; those who lack firm belief in it cannot observe them.

'But he denied and turned away' (wa lakin kadhdhaba wa tawalla) - 'Kadhdhaba' is in contrast to 'saddaqa', and 'tawalla' is in contrast to 'salla'. That is: he should have believed in the Messenger and the Hereafter, spent in the cause of Allah, and performed prayer; but instead, he chose denial and aversion.

Depicting their aversion and its cause: 'Then he went to his family, swaggering' (thumma dhahaba ila ahlihi yatamatta) - This portrays their aversion and explains its cause. The meaning is: those who possess pride in their wealth and family are not affected when warned of God and the Hereafter. They regard their prosperity and population as proof of their correctness; therefore, they do not care about the advice of those who point to their errors. Instead of being influenced, they walk away to their families, boasting that since they possess all of this, it is a proof of their prosperity. They assume the error lies with those who, despite being deprived themselves, warn them that they are on the path of ruin.
Recall that the attitude of the believers is that they remain fearful of God among their families, lest they fail to fulfill the right of guardianship and face His grasp, as stated:
(Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful [of punishment]) (At-Tur, 52:26).
In contrast, those whose hearts are devoid of His fear regard their families as a source of pride, and fall into the delusion described in the story of the garden owner in Surah Al-Kahf:
(I do not think this will ever perish) (Al-Kahf, 18:35).
The mindset of such people is described in Surah Al-Mutaffifeen as:
(And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting) (Al-Mutaffifeen, 83:31).

'Woe to you, and woe! Then woe to you, and woe!' (awla laka fa-awla, thumma awla laka fa-awla) - 'Awla' is derived from 'wayl', expressing severe rebuke, regret, and wrath. This is common in classical Arabic. Khansa wrote:
'I harbored all kinds of worries in my mind; so woe to my soul, and woe!'
Some have translated it as 'fitting' or 'deserving', which is contrary to Arabic grammar as well as context.
While the preceding verses used the third person, this addresses them directly. This change in style demonstrates the intensity of regret and wrath.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 34 and 35 was combined in the original text.]

The Surah concludes with the same theme with which it began:
Now, the Surah concludes with the same theme with which it began. At the beginning, He stated: (Does man think that We will not assemble his bones? Yes. [We are] Able to proportion his fingertips). Following that, the discourse turned to his self-will, deliberate denial, and the terror of the Resurrection. Now, He addresses that question: do those who regard being resurrected as impossible assume that human beings will be left unaccountable? If leaving him unaccountable is contrary to God's justice and wisdom, why should resurrecting him be difficult for Him? Do they not reflect on the stages of their own creation—that man is created from a drop of semen emitted into the womb? 'Yumna' (emitted) is in the passive voice, pointing to the lack of concern of the emitter, who emits the drop and walks away, having no knowledge of where it is or in what state. All subsequent developments are carried out by His power, which, within the veils of darkness, shapes it through various stages. The drop of water becomes a clinging clot, then its basic form is prepared, and then its features are proportioned, until His power brings it forth as male or female. During all these stages, His power alone shapes it; no other hand associates in it. Now, reflect: is the God Who displayed these manifestations of His power and wisdom within your own existence unable to resurrect you after death!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 36, 37, 38, 39, and 40 was combined in the original text.]
